coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical exfoliation of graphene quantum dots from waste dry cell battery for biosensor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Neeraj
Abubakar Sadique, Mohd.
Khan, Raju
coredata.dc:description: We report the preparation and performance of a novel electrochemical and luminescent material of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the electrophoretic exfoliation method. This is a cost-effective single-step method that utilizes a waste dry batteries material to produce GQDs. GQDs have been characterized for their optical property (absorbance at 279 nm) and size (avg. particle size 6–10 nm). The electrical response surface concentration (3.92 × 10-15 mol cm−2) has been done by electrochemical techniques. The results imply that the produced GQDs show promising characteristics to be used as an effective nanomaterial in biosensing and other applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste material
Electrochemical Exfoliation
GQDs
Biosensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The diagram of electrophoretic exfoliation to produce GQDs (A) ECE mechanism and (B) reactions for the GQDs exfoliation.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs. Inset fluorescence of GQDs (a) daylight (b) short-wavelength (254 nm) (c) long-wavelength (365 nm) (B) PL spectra of GQDs with different excitation (C) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs (D) XRD spectrum of GQDs (E) CV of GQDs at scan rate 20 mVs−1 (a) GC (b) GC/GQDs (F) Nyquist plots of GQDs (a) GC (b) GC/GQDs.
(A) AFM image of GQDs (2D) (B) AFM image of GQDs (3D) (C) TEM images of GQDs (D) SAED of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical exfoliation of graphene quantum dots from waste dry cell battery for biosensor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Industrial Waste Utilization, Nano and Biomaterials, CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI), Hoshangabad Road, Bhopal 462026, MP, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Industrial Waste Utilization, Nano and Biomaterials
CSIR-Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the preparation and performance of a novel electrochemical and luminescent material of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the electrophoretic exfoliation method. This is a cost-effective single-step method that utilizes a waste dry batteries material to produce GQDs. GQDs have been characterized for their optical property (absorbance at 279 nm) and size (avg. particle size 6–10 nm). The electrical response surface concentration (3.92 × 10-15 mol cm−2) has been done by electrochemical techniques. The results imply that the produced GQDs show promising characteristics to be used as an effective nanomaterial in biosensing and other applications.

ce:section.ce:para: More than 1.9 million tonnes of waste batteries are generated but only 48 % is recycled annually. Around 35 kilotons of batteries turn out as waste [1]. The Alkaline (or dry cells) batteries after due time corrode, the heavy metals inside mix with the soil and have harmful effects on human health as well as the environment. Which is a lack of recycling regulations in most of the countries worldwide. By 2022, in the dry cell market, the waste of zinc-carbon batteries will be about 92.5 % without any separation of waste for recycling [2]. The average development of dry cell batteries includes a graphite rod (GR) at the middle, which can act as an electrode [3]. A densely packed layer of single carbon atom thickness arranged in a two-dimensional sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice is termed graphene [4]. CVD-developed graphene on metal is difficult to utilize onto a useable substrate without avoiding damage to graphene sheets. Alternatively, an environmentally friendly electrochemical exfoliation method uses a functionalizing agent to produce graphene from exfoliated graphite [5]. GQDs being nanometer-sized fragments of graphene have notable luminescent behaviour. Which are associated with surface states like defects or edge effects. Electrochemical properties of GQDs align with that of graphene, making it suitable for use in electrochemical sensors as an electrode material [6]. Thus, GQDs have a boundless possibility for electrochemical as well as optical sensing [7], bioimaging [8], biosensing [9], and so forth. In the case of graphene, the exfoliation of sheets takes place from the anode due to intercalation of anions into positively charged anode usually known as electrochemical exfoliation (ECE) [10].

In this work, the electrochemical exfoliation process was employed for the synthesis of fluorescent GQDs. The characterization of GQDs was done by atomic force microscope (AFM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscope. The optical properties of GQDs were analyzed with the help of PL and UV–vis absorption spectra. The synthesized GQDs have properties to be utilized as a promising biosensing material in future diagnostics.

A waste dry battery source for Graphite rods (GRs), Citric acid (CA), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (Rankem), and Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O) (Merck), DC power supply (Powertron, Model: PIPL-30001DCPS), dialysis membrane (pore size ca. 2.4 nm, Himedia) and Milli-Q ultra-pure water as a solvent in performing all experimentations.

The synthesis of GQDs via a one-step approach is described in detail in Supporting Information. Briefly, in the exfoliation process initially, GRs were removed from two dry batteries. The electrolyte was prepared with citric acid and NaOH, and GRs were used as anode and cathode electrodes. On applying a voltage over a period, the electrolyte solution changes color to dark brown confirming the exfoliation of GR. The schematic of the (A) ECE mechanism and (B) reactions for the (GQDs) exfoliation is represented in Fig. 1 . The obtained sample was centrifuged and washed with membrane dialysis before further use (S1).

Optical properties were recorded by UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburg Instruments F900). X-ray diffraction was done on MiiFlexII Desktop X-ray Diffractometer (Rigaku). FTIR spectra were taken from 400 to 4000 cm−1 (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50). TEM for morphology were recorded by TEM (FEI, TALCOS).

Fig. 2 (A) and (B) shows the optical properties of GQDs done by PL and UV–vis absorption spectra. The UV–vis spectrum shows a definite absorption peak at ∼ 229 nm of aromatic groups resulting from the π-π* vibration [11] and another peak at ∼ 279 nm which corresponds to the signature peak of graphene. On excitation by 279 nm UV lamp, the green luminescence of GQDs was observed by UV cabinet promising their applications in fluorescence imaging. Moreover, excitation-dependent PL properties were displayed by GQDs. By changing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 440 nm, PL peak shows a redshift. The determination of functional groups in the material observed in Fig. 2 (C), the stretching vibration of C-OH bonds (3339 cm−1) in the spectra indicated the GQDs, C≡C (2119 cm−1 and C = C (1635 cm−1) [12,13]. XRD was used to characterize the graphitic crystalline nature of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (D), the formation of zero-dimensional GQD material from GR is indicated from a broad peak cantered at 2θ = 24.7° in correlation with standard data (JCPDS No. 41–1487), a broader peak at 25.15° [14], and a sharp peak at 31° corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.32 nm, which may be assigned to the graphite (002) planes. To study the electrochemical activity of the GQDs, the effect of scan rate Fig. 2 (E) shows the CV of the (b) GC/GQD modified electrode with comparison to (a) GC electrode in the presence of 3.92 × 10-15 mol cm−2 GQDs in phosphate buffer saline (10 mM, PBS, pH 7.4), containing 0.1 M KCl and 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4. Different scan rate vs. Ipa (anodic peak current) and Ipc (cathodic peak current) of GQDs were fitted by linear regression equation and found to be I (μA) = -3.722x + 2.455 (R2 = 0.99) and I (μA) = 3.209x − 2.005 (R2 = 0.996), respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements of GC and GC/GQD were carried out to optimize the charge-transfer kinetics of electrodes. The Nyquist plot in Fig. 2 (F) clearly shows the reduced resistance, the insulating, and dielectric characteristics at the interface of electrode/electrolyte affect the value of electron transfer resistance (RCT) (semicircle diameter). The RCT values for the (a) GC, (b) GC/GQDs electrodes have been obtained as 39.66 × 102 Ω and 3.50 × 102 Ω respectively. The phase shift was obtained by EIS data along with frequency calculations (Fig. S4). The surface concentrations (I*) of the GC and GC/GQD electrodes have been calculated in Supporting Information. Higher I* of GQDs enables more binding sites for enhanced performance of biosensors (S2.2).

For the structural morphology of GQDs, 2D AFM image in Fig. 3 (A) reveals that the height of the GQDs to be about −25.9 to 27.4 nm with uniform distribution of particles on the surface. Moreover, the light brown color in 3D AFM image Fig. 3 (B) reveals a uniform height distribution of the GQDs particles. The shape and size of the GQDs were characterized using TEM. The TEM images Fig. 3 (C) show that the GQDs are homogeneously distributed. The GQDs average size of GQDs is ∼10 nm. The distinctive TEM illustrations show a fairly equal size distribution of prepared GQDs between 6 and 10 nm and 6.5 nm is the lateral dimension is about (Fig. S5). SAED image Fig. 3 (D) reveals the presence of spot pattern and absence of diffuse diffraction ring pattern confirming the crystalline nature of GQDs.

The optimal use of the as-synthesized GQDs can be done in biomedical and biosensing applications. The potential of such GQD materials is vast as depicted from the properties and material characteristics. The novelty of the synthesis method has attracted many wide domains for application-based usage. The use of such high potential nanomaterials in biosensing applications has a broad scope in cancer detection [15], anti-bacterial [16], anti-viral diagnostics [17], and the field of bio-nanotechnology [18].

The facile and eco-friendly GQDs synthesized from the waste battery were achieved in the reported work. This method could be expanded to the industries for bulk GQDs synthesis. This approach is beneficial for the environment for the reason that it enables the recycling of resources and waste management (waste batteries). The main emphasis of material properties during the methodology was to optimize the synthesis parameters into a facile method to obtain a material with specific biocompatible properties (particle size ∼10 nm). This work has reported an eco-friendly, rapid, efficient synthesis route for the GQDs via the electrochemical exfoliation method. The prepared GQDs can be used in biomedical and biosensing applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere thanks to Director, CSIR-AMPRI for his encouragement and support in this work. Raju Khan would like to acknowledge SERB for providing funds in the form of the IPA/2020/000130 project and CSIR project for providing funds in the form of the MLP-0049.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.130829.

ce:section.ce:para:
